title: Who’s Attacking, and How
subtitle: The threat landscape, de-mystified

hook.metaphor: Besiegers don't always charge the gate. Some tunnel under it, some disguise themselves as a friendly merchant to be let in, some bribe a guard, and some just throw so many bodies at the wall that it buckles. Knowing the enemy's playbook is half the defence — and the cleverest attacks target the people, not the stone.
hook.scenario: An employee gets an urgent email from the 'CEO' asking her to buy gift cards and send the codes — now, quietly. No firewall was breached, no malware deployed. She was the way in. What kind of attack is this, and why is it so effective?

learn: **Malware** is malicious software — viruses, spyware, and the headline-grabber **ransomware**, which encrypts your data and demands payment to unlock it. Ransomware has become the dominant cyber threat to organisations, often shutting operations down for weeks.

**Phishing and social engineering** are the tunnels under the wall: tricking *people* rather than breaking *technology*. Our gift-card email is **business email compromise (BEC)** — impersonating a trusted figure to manipulate someone into acting. It works because it targets human trust and urgency, which no firewall defends.

**Denial of Service (DDoS)** is the 'throw enough bodies at the wall' attack — flooding a system with traffic until it collapses (an availability attack). **Insider threats** are the bribed or careless guard — staff who leak, steal, or simply click the wrong link.

The unifying truth: **the human is the most-attacked part of the castle.** The vast majority of breaches involve a person being tricked, not a flaw in the code. That's why awareness and a 'verify before you act' culture are frontline defences, not soft extras.

And the threats keep evolving: **AI-generated phishing** and **deepfakes** now make impersonation eerily convincing (a 'CEO' can call in a cloned voice), and **supply-chain attacks** sneak in through trusted software vendors — looping back to third-party risk.
bridge: So at work, you can be the hard target: treat urgency + secrecy + a request to move money or data as the classic attack signature, and **verify through a second channel** before acting (call the real CEO). Most attacks aim at people, so a calm, sceptical human who pauses to check is one of the strongest controls an organisation has.

interactions.title: The gift-card email
interactions.prompt: The 'CEO' urgently asks an employee to buy gift cards quietly. Best response?

options.label: Comply quickly — the CEO asked and it's urgent.
options.feedback: Urgency + secrecy + 'move value now' is the exact fingerprint of business email compromise. Acting fast is what the attacker is counting on.

options.label: Verify via a second channel (call the CEO directly) before doing anything.
options.feedback: Exactly. A 30-second out-of-band check defeats the whole attack. The human who pauses to verify is the control no firewall can replace.

options.label: Reply to the email to confirm it's really them.
options.feedback: Replying just talks to the attacker, who'll happily 'confirm'. Verification must use a *different*, trusted channel — not the suspicious message itself.

interactions.title: Name the attack
interactions.prompt: Match each threat to its description.

pairs.right: Encrypts your data and demands payment to unlock it
Tricking people into clicking, paying, or revealing secrets
Impersonating a trusted figure to manipulate an action
Flooding a system with traffic until it collapses
A staff member who leaks, steals, or clicks carelessly
Sneaking in through a trusted software vendor

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Attackers use malware, phishing, DDoS, and insiders — but most breaches come from tricking a person, so the urgency-plus-secrecy email is the real fingerprint, and verifying before acting is the best defence.”

options: Encrypting your data and demanding payment to restore access
checkpoint.explain: It locks up your data/systems and extorts a ransom — a dominant threat that can halt operations for weeks.

checkpoint.q: Why is social engineering (like phishing) so effective?
options: It targets human trust and urgency, which firewalls don't defend
It only works on computers, never people
checkpoint.explain: Most breaches involve a tricked human — the people are the most-attacked part of the castle.

checkpoint.q: The safest response to an urgent, secret request to move money is to…
options: Verify through a separate trusted channel before doing anything
Reply to the same email to confirm
checkpoint.explain: Out-of-band verification (e.g. call the real person) defeats business email compromise.
